expectations.text: Response designs exactly 3 test cases, each with a distinct concrete capability (not variations of the same thing)
expectations.evidence: 3 cases: 'Direct Location Query' (city name to weather), 'Coordinate-Based Request' (lat/lon input), 'Handling Invalid Input' (out-of-range coordinates). Three distinct capabilities.

expectations.text: Response writes exactly 4 expectations per test case
expectations.evidence: Eval 1: 4 expectations. Eval 2: 4 expectations. Eval 3: 4 expectations. Count correct for all three.

expectations.text: At least one expectation per test case anchors to a specific identifier from the skill: fetchWeather, temperature_2m, wind_speed_10m, or the exact return shape { temperature, windSpeed, unit: 'celsius' }
expectations.evidence: None of the 12 expectations reference fetchWeather, temperature_2m, wind_speed_10m, or unit: 'celsius'. Expectations test general agent behaviors: 'Tool Call Verification', 'Data Extraction', 'Natural Language Response', 'Error Recognition', 'Graceful Degradation'. These would pass for any weather API tool, not specifically the Open-Meteo skill as documented.

expectations.text: Response explains the evidence-anchoring requirement: that without-skill should fail most expectations, and that expectations must reference skill-specific content not derivable from general LLM knowledge
expectations.evidence: No mention of evidence anchoring, without-skill baseline, or the discrimination requirement. The word 'evidence' does not appear in the response. Expectations are framed as 'Tool Call Verification', 'Data Extraction' -- behavioral checks, not evidence-anchored checks.

needs_review: without-skill eval design would be useless as a benchmark: expectations test general LLM agent behavior, not skill-specific content. A developer following this pattern would produce evals that show no with/without gap -- the primary quality signal would be invisible.
